sentences.text: Pardon?
Yes.
Oh yeah.
I still have it.
Oh yeah, my wife and my two children.
It's just a plain ring.
All right.
No, she doesn't wear it now.
Because she has something for-- oh yeah.
Oh yeah, just a plain ring.
My wife [? weared it. ?] My daughter [? weaed it, ?] and my son [? weared it. ?]
And I was working on that machine.
And one day at-- the machine had a gauge, how many tons of power it's putting on.
And one day, I was working on the machine.
And I put the power on.
And the power shut away the maximum.
And it made a groaning noise.
And I stopped the machine.
And I call my foreman, which-- who was a German.
And I had more lucky-- I was more lucky then I even knew.
And he went over to the machine.
And he tried it.
And it didn't pull.
And he tried it again.
And that row busted.
And this row was made in Switzerland, not in Germany.
They had to pay in gold to get machinery from Switzerland, which half of the machines I worked on-- the Swiss machines made in 1944 and 1943 in Switzerland.
So the machine was broken.
Then came the SS-- all kind of interviews.
If this would've been-- if it would have happened to me, I would have been shot for sabotage.
There was no doubt in my mind or anybody else who was there at the time.
But him being a German and all that, he got away with it.
They believed it was an accident.
Because they blamed it-- if-- if the knife didn't go through, then the knife wasn't sharpened.
I came in September and I worked there-- it was-- it was a big factory.
And I did this part, and I handed away to the next, the next, the next.
And the whole factory was run by 500 maybe people.
Not the factory, but this whole-- the factory was much bigger than that.
There were thousands of people working in the factory.
I worked in the factory until March 19--
In-- in 1944, a few times, they-- we could hear planes flying, English-- or American planes.
And the rule was that we go into the barracks.
The Germans hid outside around and we were in the barracks not supposed to go out.
The factory was situated in-- in between trees in a forest.
It was country.
And one time, the Americans flew through, and they bombed the little town of Sonneberg.
And they burned down the main street from both sides-- the whole-- the whole-- the whole street, from one end to the other.
The Germans were afraid to go in and take out whatever was still capable to take out.
So they took us on a Sunday when we don't work.
And we had to go down in the cellar.
So you know, they all-- single houses, they all had a cellar.
And that was a great break for us.
Because there was food in the cellars.
You know.
So we ate.
We took some home.
We hid some, you know.
For pilfering, you could get shot.
So once in a while, we had a break, something like an extra ration or things like that.
But generally, we did not have enough to eat in Sonneberg like we had in [INAUDIBLE].
Now it came to-- it came to-- before March, something like February or something, the Germans started acting nice to us.
And they-- they-- some even offered to hide us-- you know, to not ask, but here and there, a person they kind of kept close to.
A close friend of mine, he was offered that he's gonna hide him.
But he had to get out of the camp on his own, which was no big thing.
You could get away.
You were middle of Germany.
There was no place you could go.
There was guards all over.
The-- you have no kind of papers.
You had nothing.
You had dirty clothes, all that.
By that time, it was all worn up.
Oh, you have the clothes with the-- with the stripes or you have clothes with-- with the civilian clothes, they put a [INAUDIBLE] on the back, a big patch.
So nobody went.
And-- and the mayor came down from the town.
He said he doesn't want the Jews because he's afraid they're gonna burn down the town and kill the people when-- when the Americans come.
And they-- and they-- they the Germans said that the Americans are already in-- in-- Cologne, [?
Keln. ?] I remember the-- the foreman my-- used to say, I'll be glad when they get to here.
At least I'll have a cigarette to smoke.
You know, the Americans have plenty of cigarettes.
They weren't afraid of the Americans coming.
They kind of-- they-- they wanted it, in a way.
So to make it short, they-- they said we have to go out.
So on the beginning-- no, had to be on the end of March.
They took us out, and they lined us up.
And we marched out.
And we marched all day.
We made maybe 25 kilometers or 20 miles.
And then we-- they-- they-- we came to a little town.
I don't remember the name.
But there was the [INAUDIBLE]-- the [INAUDIBLE]-- the pencils, [INAUDIBLE].
They-- the whole town worked for [INAUDIBLE].
The whole town-- it was a little town.
And after maybe five days march or something, they took us back to Sonneberg.
They said, there's no place to go.
I mean, the Americans, we get surrounded and all that.
They didn't to march.
I mean, the SS didn't want really.
They-- they had enough.
We came back into our camp.
And we found out that in the meantime, another group of marchers stayed over in our camp a couple days.
And they marched them out.
They don't want people-- so we marched again.
In Buchenwald, was the same story.
It was-- they had to march too.
So we marched for four weeks, maybe five, from the end of March to the 9th-- to the 9th of May.
We went to-- we went down to Czechoslovakia toward what they called Sudetendeutschland.
And the way we marched, we got weaker and weaker.
Food was given to us.
Then some SS man went ahead, and they get the farmers to get some food.
So if we got a couple potatoes, which was cooked in those big-- we used to call them pig-- pig-- it was a big pot, you know, where they cooked for the-- for the-- for the pigs.
It was like a boiler-- as big as a water boiler.
So you got a potato too.
It was-- was-- that was about the size of it.
And we came to one place one time.
And we each we get a free potato or whatever.
They were laid down.
And then there was a few potatoes left over.
And we all started crowding around to get the extra few potatoes.
And that SS man who was there at the time tried to get us back.
And he shot into the people.
And he shot one man from us.
He hit him in the chest.
And I don't remember his name-- so many years.
And-- and he got-- he went over to the SS man.
And he said to him, you have wounded me.
You have me wounded.
Now we marched every day out.
And some nights, we have no place to go or no farm who would let us in.
So we-- we stayed on the ground.
We all-- then before we marched out, we all took a blanket.
Because we knew we were going to march.
They told us to take a blanket.
In the beginning, they-- we had a wagon and they piled them on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we didn't pull them.
It was a truck.
And they went to the next town.
Later on, who was left behind was shot.
Through-- through the shooting, they let us march by maybe half a mile or something like that.
And one soldier was left behind.
And he's shooting.
The procedure was to take the blanket over the head.
Tell them to take the blanket over the head.
And then he shot him.
There was a couple people-- more than a couple-- who survived that.
That's how we know.
They didn't shoot them.
They shot [INAUDIBLE].
I remember one night we marched.
And there was this guy.
And he was so weak he couldn't march anymore.
And we marched-- we didn't go with the main highways because we were afraid of the American airplanes that [? we'd ?] be shoot up.
So we were between trees and we just pushed them into the trees.
You know, it was dark at night.
And he survived.
One time, I remember we sat behind on a field.
And there was like an [? abutment. ?] Like, you know, like it's going down on the ground.
And there was a big pipe coming out of it.
Must've been a pipe from refuse or whatever, you know.
And a guy climbed into that pipe.
And-- and then when-- they counted us every day.
And then when we went up-- when we stood up to march, they counted us and a guy was missing.
And how could he get away in the middle of a field with no-- so they figured out he went into the pipe.
So they shot into the pipe.
But he survived.
Because he suddenly-- the pipe bent.
It had a bend.
And he climbed-- when he heard the first shot, he-- he climbed behind the bend and he survived.
Then one time, we came to a little town.
And we went into-- it was after a hard night's working or something.
They let us into the giant barn.
And they didn't count us.
And either they-- they felt-- they forgot the count or they loss the count or whatever it was.
Next morning, when we went out, one guy hid in the barn in the rafters-- he hid someplace.
I don't know exactly where.
And they took us out.
And they counted us.
And we had a figure.
And we went maybe a mile.
And for some reason, they changed their mind.
And they took us back.
And they put us into the same barn.
And they didn't count us.
Next morning, we got up.
Eight people hid in the barn.
And they took us out, and they counted us.
They started searching in the barn.
They took us out about a half a mile from there or less.
We could still see the barn.
And we stood there.
And they knew seven people are missing.
And when they found seven people, they didn't count anymore.
The eighth saw it all.
He was up there through-- through a slot in the-- in the barn.
Somehow, he could see what's going on.
And they took them out in the yard, and they shoot them all.
And they buried them in the yard.
And they went away.
Now, after the war.
We talked to the guy.
We knew the guy.
Now, he saw it all.
And then he was sitting in the barn for three days until he couldn't take it anymore.
He was so hungry and thirsty both.
He wasn't so much hungry because there was grain laying around.
But he was thirsty.
And the Russians were already there but he didn't know that.
At night, he got out and got out of the barn.
And then he saw Russians.
Five weeks.
Now, we came into Czechoslovakia.
Before they came into Czechoslovakia, I remember we came to-- in Germany to the town of Halle.
The SS man leading us, wherever we came to-- to a point, they said they had important workers, trained laborers, they did-- vital to the armament.
We know now Hitler was dead on the 31st of April or the 30th of April.
Now, after that, it got lax.
We weren't worked so much.
But we still didn't know where to go or what to eat or anything.
So we were still matching.
And on the 8th or maybe it was the 9th-- no, it was the-- it had to be, yes, on the-- on the 9th.
On the 8th of May, they told us the war was over-- the SS men which were leading us.
The war was finished.
But they didn't give us up.
They still kept us [INAUDIBLE].
We were-- we became their protection in a way.
Because by having us, they didn't have to join the army.
So we got up in the morning.
And we marched out on a highway from a little village.
I don't remember the name.
And here comes this little, like, a tank.
It wasn't even a tank.
It had rubber wheels and-- and chains, like a-- you know, it was--
A half track-- it was a small thing.
And if I remember right, there was no more than two soldiers, two Americans in it.
And the Germans must have seen it before we-- at least before I realized what's happening.
And some of them had bicycles.
And they got on the bicycles and bicycled away.
And-- and they came down.
And we-- we are-- by that time, we had realized that this is not a German tank.
I mean, it had the big star and, you know, the color was different, the uniform was different.
And we realized that this is other soldiers.
Oh.
So I came-- they came down.
And we were about 250 left over out of the original five-something.
Most of them got killed.
Some escaped.
The majority got killed.
And they-- they said-- and they got out of the tanks.
They had whatever they had, maybe a truckload [INAUDIBLE] disappears within seconds.
I didn't get nothing.
Anyway, they didn't have-- how much can two people have?
And we didn't care about the Germans.
We didn't think this way.
I mean, we were liberated.
We didn't even realize what it means yet.
We were not-- absolutely not people anymore.
We were like-- I don't know.
I have no explanation for it.
So they piled up on those two little tanks.
This happened that we used the [INAUDIBLE] and the American pleaded with him.
And one of them could speak Czechoslovakian a little.
We could understand Czechoslovakian.
It's similar to Polish.
And he said, we cannot take you.
You have to get off.
So finally, we got off.
And they took those maybe four soldiers which didn't run away, they took him with them.
They didn't put any-- any restrictions on him.
They just took him into the half track or whether you call it.
And they drove off and left us in the middle of nowhere.
So we went into this little town, was next day.
I remember the name was Zlutice.
And we didn't know anything about Zlutice or all that.
And I still don't know this is the same Zlutice already killed all those-- where they destroyed the whole village, and they just built a town and named it Zlutice.
And that town was still under German occupation, German police with the guns, with everything.
And I said, what did we do here?
Where did we get in here?
And the war was over.
That's the 9th.
So the same story-- they didn't want the Jews in town.
It was like-- it-- it was-- it was [INAUDIBLE] that the towns were German towns.
So they bought this big [? boss. ?]
And they piled us up on the [? boss ?] with a soldier with a gun sitting in front.
And they piled us in.
And they drove us maybe, I don't know how many kilometers out of town, out of that little town.
And they stopped in the middle of nowhere.
And they let us down.
They said, go wherever you want.
They turned around and they went back.
So here we are again, the middle of no place.
So there was a fork in the road with names, you know, the-- the-- one village was one kilometer.
One village was two kilometer.
So let's-- we say, let's take the one kilometer.
It's less to walk.
So we start walking.
And here come two guys, just two guys.
One had a German overcoat from the German army.
The other was a already a civilian.
This one was a civilian too underneath the coat.
And they stop us.
We were already broken up into groups.
I took my closest friends, and we were about maybe eight, 10 people, 12 people, going at that little village.
And he says, where are you going?
And we said, we're going to this little town.
He lifts-- open up his coat.
And he lifts up a rifle which he had under the coat.
He had his hand to his pocket.
And he says, no, you're not going to this town.
We are going to this town.
I said, all right.
Those were [INAUDIBLE] officers.
They spoke Russian.
You could understand them.
And so they went-- we let them and we took the one which is two kilometers away.
So we came into the town, and we went into-- into a farm.
And we asked for some food.
They were all Germans.
And they gave us some food.
And they gave us a place in the-- in the barn we could stay.
And this must have been already, what, the 12th or the 11th?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And the farmers knew that the Russians are coming in in the area where we are.
We were liberated from the Americans, but the Americans were ahead of themselves.
And they had to go back.
So we said, where was the little-- little town?
This was a village.
There was-- so there was a next little town.
I don't remember the name-- a little, beautiful, picturesque town.
And we went in there.
And the mayor put us up in the inn.
There was an inn.
They were all Germans.
They put us up in the inn.
And we stayed in that inn until the Russians came in.
The Russians came in maybe two days later.
It took them maybe four or five days after the war for the Russians to come in.
And we went out to see the Russians coming.
And they came down, a happy lot with horses, with wagons, with tanks, with trucks, with music, with big pictures-- Stalin and Lenin and-- just like you see sometimes on the propaganda.
And they have some signs.
There's [NON-ENGLISH] that means for the-- for the fatherland, for the country, [INAUDIBLE] Stalin, for Stalin [INAUDIBLE] and all kinds.
And they came down, and-- and they-- they were told that we were there-- was about 12 of us.
And they came down.
And they treated us extraordinary.
They brought us-- they brought us whatever you can think of.
I mean, not luxuries, but you know, there was plenty of liqueurs and-- and a dead pig which was still warm.
Killed it on some farm.
And they treat us very nice.
And we started eating too fast.
And one guy died, got dysentery, couldn't take the food.
And came a doctor every day, tried everything you could.
They-- they brought-- they did everything they could-- just died.
They told us not-- not to jump on the food.
And then after a few more days came the Czechs.
I'm making this story a little short.
And they came with-- with everything prepared.
They had signs with the names of the streets in Czechoslovakian.
But they already had people to set-- to sit in this town, away from this town, and so on.
And we-- and a little short from that town was this big castle.
Supposedly, after the war, I was told that this belonged to Baron [INAUDIBLE], Baron [INAUDIBLE].
He was a big baron in the Austrian empire.
The castle had a few hundred rooms.
It had at least 200 bedrooms.
And we stayed in that castle, and we could take anything we wanted.
There were-- there were worthless things in there.
And we stayed there.
And then they sealed it, and we had to move out of there.
So we went back into this inn.
And then they started talking on the radio that we should go home.
So we took the train.
The trains were free-- I mean, for us, anyway.
And we started going home-- was slow moving trains.
On each station, they had what they called [INAUDIBLE].
That's like the Red Cross.
And they had food they handed out to anybody.
So we have no problem finding food at each station.
And we went home to Lodz.
And on the 25th of May, I was already in Lodz.
Of course, I forgot to-- to tell you-- I left out to tell you that once we got to Buchenwald, we already knew about Auschwitz and Buchenwald.
We found out because when they liquidated Auschwitz, they sent them to Buchenwald.
And the Poles who were in the uprising in 1944 in Warsaw, were also sent to Buchenwald.
So we had already news about the gas-- the gas chambers in Buchenwald-- in Auschwitz.
So when I came to Lodz, I still believed that somebody is alive.
I still-- I don't know.
Against hope, I hoped my sister might survive.
Because if she went to a camp like me, then she would have survived.
Or at least she had a 50-50 chance.
There was nobody.
I went down to the house, and it was all torn up.
The-- the Poles were looking for valuables.
They tore out the windowsills.
Those houses at that time were thick walls.
And it had a windowsill-- big, like you could sit on it, you know, like a chair, you know, the old houses.
They tore them out.
They were looking for gold or for-- maybe somebody found something valuable at someplace.
And that's all it took.
So the place which was the ghetto was half torn down or half torn apart not [INAUDIBLE] too much.
I moved in with some friends of mine into one-room apartment.
I don't know how many there were.
Maybe 20.
Who knows?
And we slept wherever we could on the floor and anything.
And-- should I go on from there?
My brother came-- my brother knew I was alive.
And he didn't know-- and each day, we went to the Jewish committee.
They hung out lists.
People sent in-- not people, but committees.
Let's say my brother was in Munich.
The Munich committee would send in name.
I don't know how, to telephone or radio.
And those names were hung out.
So I-- I saw my brother's name right there.
You're going to make another--
